text: What kind of invention is it?
subtext: Pick the closest fit for what you've created — the thing itself, not how you'd sell it.
why: What kind of invention this is changes what protection is realistic — physical products, software and processes are treated differently under IP law.

option_notes.Physical product / device: A tangible item you'd make and sell — anything from a water bottle to electronics. We'll cover patent routes, prototyping and manufacturing costs.
option_notes.Process / method: We'll look at whether a process patent is realistic and how you'd prove and defend the method.
option_notes.Composition / material: We'll look at patent options for formulations or materials, and how you'd keep the recipe protected.
option_notes.Software algorithm: We'll cover the tighter software patent rules and whether trade secret or copyright protection fits better.
option_notes.Design: We'll look at design patent or registered design routes, which protect appearance rather than function.

why: Where you are in the filing process changes what protection steps and timelines we recommend.

option_notes.No filing yet: We'll cover your filing options and the costs and timelines to get initial protection in place.
option_notes.Provisional patent filed: We'll focus on what's next — the non-provisional deadline and building out your full claims.
option_notes.Non-provisional / PCT filed: We'll focus on prosecution timelines and international filing decisions from here.
option_notes.Granted patent: We'll focus on commercialisation, licensing and enforcement rather than the filing process itself.
option_notes.Trade secret only (no filing intended): We'll focus on how you'd protect the secret operationally, since patents are off the table.

text: Who ultimately uses or buys this? Any industries you're targeting?
subtext: e.g., 'Residential electricians retrofitting older homes.' or 'Consumer pet owners.'
why: Knowing who ultimately buys or uses this shapes the market-size and licensing research in your report.

text: What's the novel claim — what does yours do that existing solutions can't?
why: The novel claim is what any patent or licensing deal would rest on — it's central to your protection strategy.
How far along your invention is changes whether we focus on validating the idea or on scaling and licensing it.

option_notes.Just an idea: We'll focus on validating feasibility and the first steps toward a prototype.
option_notes.Working prototype: We'll focus on testing, refining and the path from prototype to production.
option_notes.Small production run tested: We'll focus on scaling manufacturing and getting the invention to a wider market.
option_notes.Ready for scale / licensing: We'll focus on licensing deals, manufacturing partners and go-to-market routes.

why: Who makes it decides which cost questions follow, and whether your report covers your own build time or unit costs, minimum orders and compliance.

option_notes.I'd make it myself: We'll check home-business and safety rules for your area, and keep your cost base low with no manufacturer to pay.
option_notes.A local manufacturer: We'll cover per-unit costs, minimum order quantities, and getting samples made and quality-checked before a full production run.
option_notes.An overseas manufacturer: We'll cover per-unit costs, minimum order quantities, samples and quality control, plus freight and import duties to get stock to you.
option_notes.Not sure yet: We'll compare local vs overseas manufacturing for you, so you can see the trade-offs before deciding.

text: What will the manufacturer charge you per unit — if you already know?
subtext: Optional. Leave blank and we'll estimate manufacturing costs in your report — a real quote just makes the numbers sharper.
why: Per-unit cost is the base of your margins; we'll estimate it if blank. Example figures here are just examples — your report prices everything in your own currency, using costs for your country.

text: Roughly what will packaging and delivery cost per unit?
subtext: Optional — most people don't know this yet. Leave blank and the report estimates it.
why: Packaging and delivery are part of your per-unit cost. Rough numbers are fine — the report refines these with real local prices.

text: Roughly how much money do you have available for this?
subtext: For prototyping, filings, and getting it made — or proven — for the first time.
why: Your budget shapes whether we focus on DIY filing or a faster, funded path. Example figures here are just examples — your report prices everything in your own currency, using costs for your country.
